opportunity.title: Jeriko – an AI agent that runs directly inside your OS
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: I've been thinking about the tradeoffs between app-based agents and OS-level ones. Running an agent inside the OS does solve context persistence and resource awareness, but I worry about the sandboxing challenges—figuring out how to keep an agent's memory contained while letting it access system resources is a non-trivial security problem I'd like to hear others' thoughts on.
actions.reasoning: The post describes an AI agent with OS-level integration, which touches on system architecture and agent deployment models directly relevant to developers in this space.

opportunity.title: Show HN: ScrapAI – We scrape 500 sites. AI runs once per site, not per page
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: The "AI runs once per site, not per page" approach is actually a smart architectural pattern for scraping. Token budgets get exhausted fast if you call the model per page, but you can still get decent results by having it reason once about the site structure. I've seen similar tradeoffs when building agent systems - sometimes you want the model to have broader context rather than fragmented per-unit knowledge. Though I'm curious how they handle sites with nested paginated content without re-invoking the model.
actions.reasoning: This is developer tooling that uses AI architecture efficiently, which fits the criteria for developer tooling and AI systems.
